medications.indication_rationale: Warm compresses promote pointing and spontaneous drainage; source advised ~10 minutes four times daily.
medications.dose_route_frequency: warm compress ~10 minutes, 4 times daily (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Optional topical to the lid margin to limit secondary conjunctival infection; source used chloramphenicol 1% ointment twice daily for 5 days. Evidence for benefit in isolated hordeolum is limited.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the lid margin twice daily for 5 days (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Analgesia; source advised 1000mg up to four times daily.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg orally up to four times daily (max 4g/day) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Squeezing a facial danger-triangle lesion risks spreading infection via valveless facial veins; let it drain with heat.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a clean warm (not scalding) compress for ~10 minutes four times daily; wash hands before/after; do NOT squeeze or pop the lump.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat promotes drainage; squeezing risks dangerous spread.

patient_education_points.point: This is a stye — an infected gland at the base of an eyelash. Warm compresses several times a day help it come to a head and drain, usually within 2–3 days. Don't squeeze it.
Do not squeeze or pop it — the veins in this part of the face connect toward the brain, so squeezing can spread infection dangerously.
Go to ED / urgent eye care if the redness spreads across the whole eyelid or cheek, you get double vision or pain moving the eye, the eye bulges, your vision changes, or you develop a fever.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Drainage/resolution; residual chalazion; refer if persistent chalazion.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading periorbital redness, diplopia, painful eye movements, vision change or fever — urgent eye care/ED.

must_include_items: Preseptal/orbital cellulitis safety-net (spreading redness, diplopia, painful eye movements, vision change, fever)
Reassurance it is benign and self-limiting
acceptable_alternatives: Topical antibiotic ointment optional; simple analgesia
errors_of_commission: Advising the patient to squeeze/pop it
Prescribing oral antibiotics for an uncomplicated stye
Sending an uncomplicated stye with a negative orbital screen to ED by ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires warm-compress management, a do-not-squeeze warning, and an orbital/preseptal cellulitis safety-net.
